original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless poolId spoofing via token-pair ordering mismatch can divert swaps/liquidity into the wrong pool (funds effectively stuck/lost)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Pool identity uses keccak256(abi.encode(userToken, validatorToken)) with no canonical ordering and no validation in read helper/UX flows. If integrators/validators compute poolId with a different convention (sorted addresses) or accidentally reverse arguments, they may mint/swap against a different pool than intended. An attacker can exploit this operationally by seeding a “shadow” pool for the reversed pair, then inducing routing to the attacker-seeded pool, causing users to receive unexpected token flows and potentially trapping value in illiquid/incorrect pools.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic operational failure mode because the contract allows arbitrary pool creation and has no canonicalization. In production, routers/indexers/validators often normalize token pairs; a mismatch can be exploited without mempool visibility and without privileged access.
deployment_assumptions: At least one on-chain/off-chain integration (router, validator fee collector, UI, indexer) derives pool identifiers using a different convention than FeeAMM.getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken), e.g., sorted token addresses or reversed parameters
Users/validators follow that integration to choose pool addresses/arguments
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No router/integration code provided; risk depends on external operational components using inconsistent poolId derivation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPoolId(userToken,validatorToken)=keccak256(abi.encode(userToken,validatorToken)) with no canonical ordering; no global registry/validation of pairs

preconditions: There exists at least one intended token pair (A,B) used operationally
Operational tooling sometimes passes (B,A) or sorts addresses when it should not
Attackers can create/seeding liquidity in the unintended direction-pool (B,A) to make it appear usable

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Different components may treat the pair as unordered, while FeeAMM treats it as ordered; shadow pools can exist for both (A,B) and (B,A)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persistent once any integration mismatch exists

operational_questions: Do validators/Tempo payment flows always call FeeAMM with the same (userToken, validatorToken) ordering as used for mint/burn accounting?
Do any indexers/routers normalize/sort token addresses for pool lookup?
Are there monitoring/alerts for duplicate pools on reversed pairs?
validation_requirements: Review operational code that selects pool arguments and calls FeeAMM
Scan chain state for both (A,B) and (B,A) pools being used for the same intended pair
Simulate integration calls with swapped arguments and quantify user impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create a shadow pool for reversed pair (B,A) via minting minimal liquidity (if any LP tooling will route to it)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Rely on integration mismatch to route swaps/mints to the shadow pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit user confusion/illiquidity: users may be unable to exit due to poor reserves or may receive wrong token outputs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally cycle liquidity between pools to exacerbate availability failures
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low on-chain coordination; main requirement is an observable integration mismatch

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: fee conversions and liquidity operations may go to unintended pools, causing settlement failures and user/validator accounting breaks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: may require operational migration, user education, and/or deploying a new AMM with canonical pair handling
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pool interactions that depend on consistent pair identification (liquidity provisioning, fee swaps, rebalancing checks)

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate (understanding of poolId derivation and integration behavior)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime; does not rely on transaction ordering

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Existence of duplicate pools for the same token pair in reverse order; liquidity distributed across both
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed fee conversion settlements; unexpected pool reserves in monitoring dashboards
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Swaps/mints occurring on a pool with reversed ordering relative to documented operational intent

validation_result.mechanism: ordered poolId in asymmetric AMM design
validation_result.impact_summary: No code defect exists. The ordered poolId is intentional because userToken and validatorToken play asymmetric roles in the FeeAMM design.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that getPoolId lacks canonical ordering and this constitutes a spoofing vulnerability. However, FeeAMM is an asymmetric AMM where userToken and validatorToken have fundamentally different roles:

1. executeFeeSwap: caller sends userToken, receives validatorToken (0.3% fee)
2. rebalanceSwap: caller sends validatorToken, receives userToken (0.15% fee)
3. mint: only validatorToken is deposited
4. burn: both tokens returned proportionally

A pool for (tokenA-as-userToken, tokenB-as-validatorToken) is semantically distinct from (tokenB-as-userToken, tokenA-as-validatorToken) because the swap directions, fee application, and liquidity provision mechanics differ. Having getPoolId(A,B) != getPoolId(B,A) is the correct design for an ordered/directional pair.

All internal functions consistently use the same (userToken, validatorToken) ordering. There is no internal inconsistency within the contract. The hypothesis depends entirely on external integrations using a different convention, which is an integration concern, not a code defect in the smart contract.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 52-57 of FeeAMM.sol show getPoolId using keccak256(abi.encode(userToken, validatorToken)). Every function that computes a poolId passes the parameters in the same order they receive them: executeFeeSwap(userToken, validatorToken, ...) calls getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken), rebalanceSwap does the same, mint does the same, burn does the same, getPool does the same, checkSufficientLiquidity does the same. The contract is internally consistent. The asymmetric token roles (userToken is deposited via executeFeeSwap, validatorToken is deposited via mint/rebalanceSwap) make ordered identification correct by design.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: NOT VULNERABLE: The ordered hashing is appropriate because the FeeAMM treats token pairs directionally. userToken and validatorToken have distinct roles (different fee rates, different deposit/withdrawal mechanics). Pool (A,B) and pool (B,A) represent genuinely different economic configurations. Canonical ordering would be incorrect here as it would conflate two semantically different pools.

calling_sequence: 2. All state-mutating functions (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint, burn) receive (userToken, validatorToken) as parameters and pass them to getPoolId in the same order
6. These asymmetric roles make ordered poolId the correct design
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable code path exists. The hypothesis requires external integrations to use a different convention, which is an off-chain integration concern, not a smart contract defect.

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one on-chain/off-chain integration derives pool identifiers using a different convention than FeeAMM.getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken)
Users/validators follow that integration to choose pool addresses/arguments
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts or integration code provided. The foundry.toml only configures the Solidity compiler and dependencies. No router, UI, indexer, or validator integration code is available for review.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario requires external integrations to compute poolId differently (e.g., sorting addresses). Even if this occurred, it would not constitute a code defect in FeeAMM.sol—it would be a bug in the integration. The FeeAMM contract correctly identifies pools by their ordered (userToken, validatorToken) pair, which is semantically meaningful given the asymmetric roles. An attacker 'seeding a shadow pool' for (B,A) when the intended pool is (A,B) would just create a different pool with reversed economic semantics; users interacting with the correct contract functions and correct parameter ordering would never be affected. The scenario requires a broken external integration as a prerequisite.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact on the FeeAMM contract itself. If external integrations incorrectly reverse token ordering, those integrations would interact with non-existent or different pools, likely causing transactions to revert (due to zero reserves) rather than silently routing to wrong pools. This is a documentation/integration concern, not a smart contract vulnerability.
recommended_mitigations: Document clearly that pool identity is directional: getPoolId(A,B) != getPoolId(B,A)
Provide integration SDKs/libraries that correctly derive poolId using the same convention
Consider adding NatSpec documentation on getPoolId emphasizing parameter ordering matters
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FeeAMM. 2. Call getPoolId(tokenA, tokenB) and getPoolId(tokenB, tokenA), confirm they differ. 3. Verify all contract functions consistently use the same ordering internally. 4. Confirm that interacting with pool (A,B) using executeFeeSwap(A,B,...) works correctly. 5. Confirm that calling executeFeeSwap(B,A,...) accesses a different (likely empty) pool and reverts on insufficient reserves.
uncertainties: External integration code is not available for review; it is possible that off-chain components use inconsistent ordering, but this would be an integration bug not a contract defect
additional_testing_needed: Review any router/integration contracts that call FeeAMM to verify they use consistent (userToken, validatorToken) ordering
Check UI/frontend code for correct parameter ordering when calling FeeAMM functions
